slides.title: Networking Foundations: IP, OSI, TCP/UDP
bullets: 🌐 IP addresses act as digital IDs for devices—IPv4 (32-bit, ~4B addresses) vs IPv6 (128-bit, ~340 undecillion addresses) solves exhaustion.
🧱 The OSI model is a 7-layer conceptual framework that standardizes network communication—each layer handles a specific function from physical bits to user apps.
🔌 TCP is connection-oriented, reliable, and ordered—ideal for data integrity (e.g., web pages, emails); UDP is connectionless, fast, and best-effort—ideal for real-time (e.g., video calls, DNS).
🧭 DNS translates human-friendly names (e.g., google.com) into machine-readable IP addresses through a distributed, hierarchical lookup process.
🛡️ Understanding these fundamentals builds a security- and scalability-first mindset—critical for designing resilient systems.
slides.example: Imagine ordering pizza: TCP is like calling a pizzeria with a confirmation number—you wait, they repeat back your order, and if something’s missed, they re-send it. UDP is like shouting your order across a noisy street—you get it out fast, but if the cook misses part of it, too bad—no second chance.
slides.selfTestQuestion: 🤔 Why would you choose UDP over TCP for a live video streaming app, even though UDP doesn’t guarantee delivery?
slides.speakerNotes:
Alright, let’s dive into the invisible plumbing of the internet—because before you scale a system like WhatsApp or Netflix, you *must* understand how data actually moves. 

First up: IP addresses. Think of them as the digital equivalent of street addresses. Without them, your laptop, phone, or server would be like a house with no address—messages couldn’t find it. IPv4, our old workhorse, uses 32 bits—so only about 4.3 billion unique addresses. Sounds like a lot… until you realize we’ve got *billions* of devices per person now! That’s why IPv6 was born: 128 bits, giving us more addresses than grains of sand on Earth. It’s not just ‘more’—it’s *astronomical* scalability. And remember: public IPs (like your router’s face to the internet) vs. private IPs (like your phone’s ID on your home Wi-Fi) help us conserve space and secure traffic internally.

Now, the OSI model—seven layers, but don’t panic! It’s not a rigid spec; it’s a *thinking tool*. Imagine building a house: the physical layer is the bricks and wires (layer 1), the data link layer is how rooms are wired together (layer 2), the network layer is GPS routing (layer 3), transport is managing moving trucks and deliveries (layer 4), session is scheduling when movers show up (layer 5), presentation is translating blueprints from French to English (layer 6), and application? That’s you, telling your contractor what kind of kitchen you want (layer 7). Even though modern stacks like TCP/IP don’t map 1:1 to OSI, this model helps us *diagnose* issues: Is your app slow because of encryption (presentation layer)? Or is the router dropping packets (network layer)?

Which brings us to TCP vs UDP—two titans of transport. TCP is the meticulous accountant: handshake, acknowledge, retransmit, reorder. Perfect for bank transfers or downloading a PDF. But that reliability has *latency cost*. UDP? The sprinter—no handshake, no guarantees, just fire-and-forget. Why use it? Because in live gaming or video calls, a delayed packet is *worse* than a missing one. If your Zoom call lags because a frame arrived late, you’d rather skip that frame than wait for it. That’s the *latency vs. reliability* tradeoff—and great system designers *know* when to break the rules.

Finally, DNS—the internet’s phonebook. You type ‘youtube.com’—your browser doesn’t know where to go. It asks a local resolver, which checks its cache, then hits root servers, TLD servers (.com), and finally authoritative servers—like a detective chasing clues—to get the IP. Without DNS? We’d all be memorizing IP addresses like 216.58.214.206… and we’d all be terrible at remembering things. DNS is why the internet feels *human*.

So—why does this all matter? Because when your app goes viral, when the cloud crashes, when security’s breached—you’ll know *where* to look. These aren’t just theory—they’re the bedrock of every scalable, secure, and fast system you’ll ever design.

slides.title: How DNS Works & Load Balancing
bullets: 🔍 DNS resolution is a recursive journey: resolver → root → TLD → authoritative server → client
🌐 Root, TLD, and authoritative servers form DNS’s hierarchical backbone—each with distinct roles and oversight (ICANN/IANA)
📝 DNS records (A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, etc.) store domain instructions and include TTL for cache expiration
🔄 Load balancing distributes traffic across servers using Layer 4 (IP/port) or Layer 7 (content-aware) routing
⚙️ Software vs. hardware load balancers: flexibility vs. performance—cloud services like Route53 blur the line
slides.example: When you type example.com, your browser first asks a DNS resolver, which recursively queries root → .com TLD → example.com’s authoritative server to get its IP—then the browser connects to load a webpage.
slides.selfTestQuestion: 🤔 If a DNS resolver has a cached A record with TTL=300 and 120 seconds have passed, what happens when a new query arrives for the same domain before TTL hits zero?
slides.speakerNotes:
Alright, let’s walk through the magic behind every URL you type—because behind that simple ‘example.com’ is a beautifully orchestrated global system. Imagine you’re ordering coffee: you (the client) walk into a café and ask the barista (the DNS resolver) for a latte. The barista doesn’t keep every coffee recipe memorized forever—they check their mental notepad (cache). If it’s not there, they go ask the head trainer (root server), who says, ‘Look under the coffee section—ask the TLD barista for .com drinks.’ So the barista goes to the .com TLD counter, who replies, ‘For example.com, talk to the baristas at ns1.example.com.’ Finally, they ask the *authoritative* baristas (the domain’s own nameserver), who hand them the exact recipe: IP address 93.184.216.34. They rush back to you with the latte—err, the IP—and your browser says, ‘Perfect, now fetch the webpage!’

Now, who runs this? The root servers—13 *types*, but over 1,000 *instances* worldwide using Anycast routing—are managed by ICANN. Think of them as the world’s most reliable phonebook index. TLD servers? Run by IANA (ICANN’s arm)—they split domains into generic (.com, .org) and country-coded (.uk, .jp). And authoritative servers? They’re the actual custodians of your domain’s truth—like Google’s servers for google.com.

DNS records are the fine print of this system. An A record? That’s your IP address—your home address. AAAA? IPv6’s version. CNAME? A forwarding note: ‘This alias points there.’ MX? ‘Send mail here.’ And TTL? That’s the freshness label—like milk on a carton. If TTL is 300 seconds and 120 passed? The resolver *still uses the cached record*—no re-query needed. Only when it hits zero does it refresh.

Now—what if traffic floods in? That’s where load balancing comes in. Picture a busy subway station with multiple turnstiles. A load balancer is the station manager rerouting crowds so no turnstile jams. Layer 4? It only sees IP and port—like counting people by shirt color. Layer 7? It reads the *entire* ticket—knows if you’re heading to the museum or concert—and routes accordingly. Software load balancers? Think open-source apps you customize. Hardware? Dedicated boxes—expensive but lightning-fast.

And reverse DNS? That’s like asking, ‘Who lives at 123 Main St?’—used by email servers to spot spam. Not mandatory, but a trust signal.

So next time a site loads instantly—remember: DNS is a global relay race, and load balancing is the traffic director keeping everything smooth. You’re not just typing a URL—you’re triggering a symphony of distributed intelligence.

slides.title: DNS Load Balancing & Clustering
bullets: 🌍 DNS load balancing distributes traffic across servers via DNS records but lacks real-time health awareness—no automatic failover for downed servers.
🔄 Common routing algorithms include Round-robin, Weighted Round-robin, Least Connections, Least Response Time, and Hashing—each balancing differently based on server state or request attributes.
🛡️ Load balancing enhances scalability, redundancy, and efficiency—but requires redundant load balancers (e.g., active/passive clusters) to avoid single points of failure.
🧩 Clustering groups servers into coordinated units (e.g., Kubernetes, Cassandra clusters), enabling high availability and performance, with HA configurations like active-active or active-passive.
⚡ Caching dramatically accelerates data access using hierarchical memory (L1–L3), but requires smart eviction policies (e.g., LRU, LFU) and careful invalidation to maintain consistency.
slides.example: Imagine DNS load balancing like a receptionist routing calls to fixed phone numbers—even if one employee’s desk is empty, calls still go there, causing missed connections. In contrast, a smart load balancer checks if desks are occupied before routing.
slides.selfTestQuestion: 🤔 Why would a hot cache hit be faster than a cold one—and what layer of memory hierarchy does each typically involve?
slides.speakerNotes:
Alright, let’s unpack this step by step—because understanding how traffic gets routed to your servers is foundational to building resilient, scalable systems. First up: DNS load balancing. At first glance, it’s elegant—just configure multiple A or AAAA records for a domain, and DNS rotates through them. But here’s the catch: DNS doesn’t *know* your servers are down. If Server C crashes, clients still get its IP and timeout—sometimes for *minutes*, due to DNS caching in resolvers and OSes. That’s why DNS-based load balancing is best suited for *geo-distributed failover*, not real-time traffic shaping.

Now, for *actual* load balancing—where servers *are* monitored—we use dedicated load balancers (hardware or software like Nginx or HAProxy) that apply algorithms. Round-robin is simple but naive—equal shares, even if one server is a powerhouse and another’s struggling. Weighted round-robin fixes that by letting you assign, say, a weight of 3 to a beefy server and 1 to a smaller one. But even better: *least connections*—it tracks active sessions and routes to the least busy server. *Least response time* goes further, factoring in latency *and* connection count. And *hashing*? That’s how we achieve sticky sessions—route based on client IP, so a logged-in user keeps hitting the same backend.

But wait—what if *the load balancer itself* fails? That’s where clustering saves the day. Think of it like a relay team: in active-active mode, two load balancers share traffic—double throughput, zero downtime. In active-passive, one’s on duty while the other stands by, ready to leap in during a healthcheck failure. Kubernetes does this with keepalived or built-in HA modes.

Now, onto caching—the unsung hero of performance. Remember Phil Karlton’s quip? Cache invalidation *is* hard. Why? Because if data changes but the cache isn’t invalidated, users get stale responses—think a user updating their profile, then seeing the old name. Caches use hierarchies: L1 is tiny but lightning-fast (CPU-level), L2 bigger and slightly slower, L3 shared and still faster than RAM. A *hot* cache hit pulls from L1—nanoseconds. A *cold* hit might pull from L3—still fast, but microseconds. A *miss*? That’s when you hit the database, and latency spikes.

Eviction policies decide what to throw out when the cache fills up. FIFO? Just kicks out the oldest—ignores that Tuesday’s trending article is still popular. LRU? Keeps recently used items—great for most web apps. LFU? Prioritizes frequency—ideal for content like YouTube videos where a few hits go viral. But here’s the kicker: *distributed caching* (like Redis Cluster) adds network complexity—you must sync writes across nodes, handle partition tolerance, and avoid split-brain scenarios. So yes—caching is powerful, but it’s not free. You trade consistency for speed, and that tradeoff must be intentional.

So—how do these pieces fit together? Imagine Netflix: DNS routes users to the nearest regional endpoint. There, a load balancer (say, Nginx) distributes streams across thousands of servers using least connections. Behind them, a Redis cluster caches video metadata and user preferences—using LRU eviction. And if a cache node dies? Its replicas take over—thanks to clustering. It’s a symphony of techniques, each playing its part.

slides.title: Caching, CDNs, Proxies & Availability
bullets: ⚡ Distributed caches pool RAM across servers to create a fast, scalable in-memory store—ideal for repeated-read workloads like database query results or session data.
🌐 CDNs distribute static assets across edge servers worldwide, drastically reducing latency for global users (e.g., a UK visitor fetching your US-hosted site from London).
🛡️ Proxies act as intermediaries: forward proxies shield clients (e.g., anonymizing web browsing), while reverse proxies protect and optimize backend servers (e.g., Nginx handling SSL + caching).
⏱️ Availability is measured in 'nines'—99.9% (three nines) allows only ~8.8 hours downtime/year, but *sequence* of components drags down overall availability, while *parallel* redundancy boosts it.

⚖️ Scalability isn’t just about adding servers: vertical scaling (bigger machines) is simple but risky; horizontal scaling (more machines) improves fault tolerance but introduces complexity like data inconsistency.
slides.example: A UK user visiting a US-hosted e-commerce site: instead of waiting for a transatlantic request, they receive cached product images and CSS from Cloudflare’s London edge node—cutting load time from 800ms to 45ms.
slides.selfTestQuestion: 🤔 If two services each have 99.5% availability and are deployed in *sequence*, what’s the approximate combined availability—and why would deploying them in *parallel* yield dramatically better uptime?
slides.speakerNotes:
Alright, let’s unpack this. Imagine you’re building the next big social platform—millions of users, global scale, zero tolerance for lag. First, what’s a *distributed cache*? It’s not just Redis on one box. Think of it like a shared memory bank: instead of each app server hitting the database for the same user profile, they all ask a unified cache cluster—spread across multiple machines—where hot data lives in RAM. That means sub-millisecond reads, even under heavy load. But here’s the catch: caching only shines when requests repeat—like trending hashtags or popular product listings. If every user searches something unique (low repetition), or data changes every second (like stock prices), caching becomes a liability—you’ll waste memory and fight cache invalidation. And remember: caches are *transient*. Never store your user’s payment info there—RAM vanishes on reboot!

Now, CDNs: they’re like regional warehouses for your static content. Say you host a video course on servers in Virginia. Without a CDN, a student in Sydney downloads each video through a 250ms round-trip—slow and expensive. With Cloudflare or CloudFront, the video is cached in Sydney’s edge location. Now it’s 15ms. The origin server barely breaks a sweat. Two CDN strategies: *Push* (you upload updates manually—great for evergreen content like docs), and *Pull* (content auto-caches on first request—perfect for viral news sites). But watch out: if your audience is in a country with no CDN nodes? You’re worse off than before.

Proxies—ah, the unsung heroes. A *forward proxy* is like a proxy for *you* (e.g., your company’s firewall filtering outbound traffic or letting you bypass geo-blocks). A *reverse proxy* is for *your servers*—Nginx in front of your app, handling TLS termination, compressing responses, caching API results, and distributing load. Crucially: a reverse proxy can *act* as a load balancer, but a basic load balancer can’t do caching or SSL offload. Don’t confuse them!

Availability—let’s talk nines. 99.9% sounds great until you realize that’s 8.76 hours of downtime *per year*—enough to miss a major product launch. And here’s the math trap: if your auth service (99.9%) and payment service (99.9%) are *in sequence*, total availability is 99.8%—*worse* than either alone! But if they’re *in parallel* (e.g., two auth servers), availability becomes 99.9999%—that’s *seconds* of downtime per year. That’s redundancy in action.

Finally, scaling: vertical scaling is like upgrading your laptop from 8GB to 32GB RAM—simple, but you’ll hit a ceiling and risk total failure if that one beast dies. Horizontal scaling—adding more laptops—is how you survive Black Friday traffic. But now you need consistent data (hello, distributed databases), smarter routing, and you’ll stress your database more. Trade-offs, always trade-offs. The goal? Balance speed, resilience, and cost—without mistaking a cache for your database’s memory.

slides.title: RAID, Storage Systems & Databases
bullets: 💾 RAID combines multiple disks for performance or redundancy—levels like RAID 0 (striping), RAID 1 (mirroring), RAID 5/6 (parity), and RAID 10 (hybrid) each balance speed, cost, and fault tolerance differently.
📁 Storage types: File (hierarchical, e.g., EFS), Block (chunked with IDs, e.g., cloud volumes), and Object (scalable, e.g., S3) serve distinct use cases from user files to high-performance apps.
🌐 NAS provides on-premises network storage, while HDFS enables reliable, distributed storage for massive datasets using block replication across clusters.
📊 Databases organize data via schemas, tables, rows/columns—and rely on DBMS for management, optimization, and ACID/BASE guarantees.

⚡ SQL databases (e.g., PostgreSQL) enforce structure and consistency but scale vertically; NoSQL types (e.g., MongoDB, DynamoDB) offer flexibility and horizontal scaling for modern, high-velocity workloads.
slides.example: A RAID 1 array with two 1TB drives gives you 1TB usable capacity but protects against single-drive failure—like having a backup copy of your entire hard drive on a second drive, ready to take over instantly.
slides.selfTestQuestion: 🤔 If you need to tolerate up to two simultaneous drive failures with high read performance and 80% capacity utilization, which RAID level should you choose?
slides.speakerNotes:
Alright, let’s dive into storage and databases—two foundational pillars of modern computing. First, RAID: it’s not just about backups; it’s about *resilience* and *performance engineering*. Imagine you’re building a library—would you rather have one rare-book vault (single disk), or duplicate every shelf in two identical rooms (RAID 1), or spread each book across shelves in three rooms with a checksum page stored differently in each (RAID 5)? That’s the intuition. RAID 0? That’s like splitting a 1000-page book into 100-page chunks and storing them across 5 shelves—you get lightning-fast reading, but if *any* shelf burns down? Gone. Poof. No recovery. RAID 5 uses parity—like adding a checksum page per stripe—to rebuild lost data from remaining disks. RAID 6 doubles that safety net, letting you lose *two* drives—critical for large arrays where rebuilds take hours and second failures are risky. And RAID 10? Think of it as two mirrored pairs, then striping across them—fast like RAID 0, safe like RAID 1—but at 50% capacity cost. It’s the go-to for mission-critical apps like databases where uptime and speed both matter.

Now, storage *types*. File storage? That’s your familiar folder structure—perfect for shared documents. Block storage? That’s raw, uninterpreted chunks—ideal for VMs or databases that need low-level control. Object storage? That’s the cloud’s backbone: immutable, scalable, and metadata-rich—great for backups, media, logs. Each has trade-offs: file is user-friendly but not high-performance; block is fast but complex; object is infinitely scalable but higher latency.

NAS and HDFS? NAS is like a big, shared external hard drive on your LAN—great for small teams. HDFS? That’s for *big data*—think petabytes across thousands of machines. It assumes hardware *will* fail, so it replicates blocks (usually 3x) and prioritizes throughput over low latency. It’s not for real-time apps—it’s for batch processing, analytics, ML training.

Finally, databases. SQL vs NoSQL isn’t just syntax—it’s *philosophy*. SQL says: structure first, consistency always. ACID means your money transfers never vanish. NoSQL says: scale and agility first. BASE says: eventually consistent, but you’ll handle 10x more requests. A materialized view? That’s like pre-making a report instead of running the query every time—huge for dashboards. The N+1 problem? Classic ORM pitfall: fetching 100 posts, then doing 100 extra queries to get each author’s name. Ouch. Fix it with joins or batch loading.

So—what RAID level tolerates *two* drive failures *and* gives ~80% capacity? RAID 6! It stripes data + parity across ≥4 disks, with *double* parity for dual-drive resilience. Perfect for archival or large storage arrays where rebuild risk is high. But remember: RAID isn’t backup—it’s *availability*. You still need backups. Got it?

slides.title: Multi-Model & Database Fundamentals
bullets: 🧠 Multi-model databases integrate multiple data models (e.g., relational, graph, document) into one backend for flexible, complex data handling.
📊 SQL vs NoSQL: SQL offers structured schemas, ACID compliance, and complex joins; NoSQL prioritizes scalability, flexible schemas, and horizontal scaling.
🔄 Replication enhances reliability and read performance—master-slave (read/write split) vs master-master (dual write support) each with trade-offs.
🔍 Indexes accelerate reads via pointer-based lookups but incur write overhead—dense indexes (every row indexed) vs sparse (subset, faster writes).
🧱 Normalization eliminates redundancy and anomalies (insert/update/delete) through structured schema design (1NF to BCNF).
slides.example: ArangoDB, a multi-model database, stores user profiles as documents, their social connections as a graph, and session metadata as key-value—all in one system.
slides.selfTestQuestion: 🤔 Why would you avoid using a fully normalized 3NF schema in a high-throughput analytics application?
slides.speakerNotes:
Alright, let’s dive into a fascinating evolution in database architecture: multi-model databases—and how they fit into the broader landscape of SQL, NoSQL, replication, indexing, and normalization. 

First, imagine you’re building a platform like a social network with user profiles, friendship graphs, real-time messaging, and event logs. You *could* spin up four separate databases—PostgreSQL for profiles, Neo4j for friendships, Redis for sessions, and Elasticsearch for logs. But that’s messy: you’d need complex application-level orchestration, risk data inconsistency, and juggle multiple query languages and backup systems. Enter multi-model databases like ArangoDB or Azure Cosmos DB—they unify these models *under one roof*. Think of it like a Swiss Army knife instead of five separate tools. They let you store a user as a document, their friends as a graph edge, and their login tokens as key-value pairs—all while maintaining consistency and atomicity across operations. That’s flexibility *without fragmentation*.

Now, where does this fit in the classic SQL vs NoSQL debate? SQL databases—like PostgreSQL or MySQL—are your structured, ACID-compliant workhorses. They enforce rigid schemas (every row *must* have a name, email, etc.), support complex joins, and guarantee transactional safety—ideal for banking or ERP systems. NoSQL databases—MongoDB, Cassandra, DynamoDB—sacrifice some ACID guarantees to gain horizontal scalability and dynamic schemas. Need to add a new field to a user profile? In NoSQL, just write it—no migration needed. Perfect for agile startups or IoT data ingestion where data volume and velocity are extreme.

But scalability isn’t just about choosing SQL or NoSQL—it’s about *how* you scale. Vertical scaling (bigger servers) hits hardware limits fast and gets prohibitively expensive. Horizontal scaling (adding commodity servers) is where NoSQL shines—and replication makes it possible. Let’s talk replication: in master-slave, one node handles writes, others replicate reads. Great for read-heavy apps like news sites—but if the master dies, writes halt until failover. Master-master fixes that: both nodes accept writes, enabling seamless failover. But here’s the catch: conflict resolution. If two users update the same profile simultaneously across continents, who wins? You’ll need eventual consistency or conflict-free replicated data types (CRDTs), adding complexity.

Then there’s indexing—the unsung hero of performance. An index is like a book’s index: instead of reading every page, you jump to page 42. But every time you insert or update, the index must be rewritten. Dense indexes store an entry for *every* row—great for random lookups, but memory-hungry. Sparse indexes only index, say, every 10th row—faster writes, but after the binary search, you scan a few pages to find the exact record. Trade-offs everywhere!

Finally, normalization. Why go through the hassle of splitting tables? Because unnormalized data breeds anomalies. Take our earlier example: a flat table with ID, Name, Role, Team. If you delete Team B, you *lose* Brian’s role and name—deletion anomaly. If Hailey appears twice (duplicate), updating her promotion requires touching two rows—update anomaly. If a new hire has no team yet? You *can’t* insert them—insert anomaly. Normalization fixes this by creating separate tables (Users, Teams) linked by foreign keys. First normal form (1NF) says: no repeating groups, each cell one value. Second (2NF): eliminate partial dependencies (e.g., team name shouldn’t depend on just part of a composite key). Third (3NF): remove transitive dependencies (e.g., team location shouldn’t depend on team name—it depends on team ID). The result? A schema that’s flexible, anomaly-free, and future-proof—even if it means more joins in queries. (Though in high-write systems, *denormalization*—intentionally duplicating data—can be a performance win.)

So—multi-model, SQL vs NoSQL, replication, indexing, normalization—they’re not isolated topics. They’re tools. Your job as an architect? Match the right tool to the job. Need ACID and complex relationships? Go relational. Need global low-latency writes? Try Cosmos DB with multi-model capabilities. Building a real-time leaderboard? Redis with sorted sets and replication. The database is the foundation—choose wisely, and your app will scale, evolve, and endure.

slides.title: Normalization, Denormalization & Consistency Models
bullets: ⚠️ Normalization reduces redundancy but adds complexity and slows queries due to joins.
⚡ Denormalization improves read performance by adding controlled redundancy—*not* reversing normalization.
🔄 ACID ensures strict consistency and reliability (atomicity, consistency, isolation, durability).
🌐 BASE offers relaxed consistency for scalability (basically available, soft state, eventual consistency).
⚖️ CAP/PACELC force trade-offs: choose Consistency vs Availability under partitions, or latency vs consistency in normal operation.
slides.example: A social media app might denormalize user posts and comments to speed feed rendering—even if it risks showing slightly outdated comment counts—because users prioritize fast loading over absolute real-time accuracy.
slides.selfTestQuestion: 🤔 If your distributed database chooses CP (Consistency + Partition Tolerance), what happens to availability during a network partition?
slides.speakerNotes: Alright, let’s unpack this crucial intersection of database design, consistency, and distributed systems—because real-world apps *never* live in textbook-perfect conditions. First, normalization: yes, it’s the gold standard for eliminating redundancy and ensuring referential integrity. But let’s be honest—perfect normalization is like aiming for 100% fuel efficiency in a sports car: theoretically ideal, but practically limiting. In practice, you *will* violate normalization rules—maybe to denormalize a frequently joined table, or to avoid costly cross-database joins in a sharded architecture. The key? Anticipate the trade-offs: redundant data means more writes to keep in sync, and inconsistent dependencies can creep in if your app doesn’t enforce integrity at the application layer. Think of it like managing a team: you delegate tasks (split data across tables), but if communication breaks down (no joins), you need redundancy—like shared notes—so everyone stays aligned. But those notes? They can get outdated. That’s denormalization: intentional redundancy to avoid expensive coordination. It’s not ‘undoing’ normalization—it’s strategic compromise. Now, consistency models: ACID is your bank transfer—either both accounts update, or *nothing* changes. It’s reliable but rigid. BASE is more like social media likes: you see a number, it might be off by 2, but it updates over time—and the app stays responsive even during outages. The CAP theorem tells us: in a distributed world, you *must* tolerate partitions (network splits), so you pick *either* consistency (everyone sees the same data *now*) *or* availability (the system always responds, even if stale). PostgreSQL? CP—when the network glitches, it *shuts down* non-consistent nodes to avoid lies. Cassandra? AP—it keeps serving data, even if it’s outdated, and syncs later. And PACELC adds the *real* kicker: even *without* partitions, you’re choosing between low latency and strong consistency. Because what good is consistency if your users wait 30 seconds for a response? Finally, distributed transactions—like moving money across two banks—require heavy coordination. Two-phase commit? Great, but if the coordinator crashes mid-commit? You’re stuck. That’s why modern systems often use sagas: break the transaction into local steps with compensating actions (like refunding a failed reservation). The bottom line? There’s no universal ‘best’—just the *right* trade-off for your use case. Ask yourself: is data integrity non-negotiable (go ACID), or is speed and uptime more critical (go BASE)?

slides.title: Sharding & Consistent Hashing Deep Dive
bullets: 🔍 Sharding splits large databases horizontally into independent shards with identical schemas.
🔄 Consistent hashing minimizes data movement during scaling by placing keys/nodes on a virtual ring.
🧩 Virtual nodes (VNodes) distribute load evenly and prevent hotspots by mapping physical nodes multiple times.
🛡️ Federation offers logical unity across heterogeneous databases without tight coupling.
⚠️ Sharding introduces complexity: cross-shard joins, rebalancing overhead, and debugging challenges (e.g., Saga patterns).
slides.example: In Apache Cassandra, consistent hashing with VNodes ensures even data distribution and seamless node additions without full data redistribution.
slides.selfTestQuestion: 🤔 If you add one node to a 10-node cluster using naive modulo-based hashing, approximately what % of keys would need reassignment? How does consistent hashing improve this?
slides.speakerNotes:
Alright, folks—let’s unpack one of the most powerful—and *tricky*—patterns in distributed systems: sharding. Imagine you’ve built a killer app, and suddenly you’re handling millions of requests per second. Your monolithic database? It’s groaning under the weight. Vertical scaling—throwing more CPU and RAM at one machine—hits a hard wall. That’s where sharding comes in: it’s like splitting a massive library into smaller, specialized branches, each holding a unique subset of books but following the same cataloging system. Each branch is a *shard*, and every shard has the same schema—same columns, same structure—but only a portion of the data. This horizontal partitioning isn’t just about scale; it’s about resilience. If one shard goes down, the others keep serving traffic. It’s like having multiple servers in different regions: localized failure doesn’t bring down the whole system.

But here’s the rub: *how* do you decide which shard a given request goes to? A naive approach is to hash the key—say, user ID—and take modulo N, where N is the number of shards. Sounds clean, right? But add one shard? Now N changes, and *poof*—most keys remap. You’re redistributing 90% of your data just to scale up. Ouch. That’s why consistent hashing was invented. Think of it like a circular hash ring: both keys and nodes are placed on the ring using the same hash function. A request goes to the *next* node clockwise. When you add a new node, only the keys *between* the new node and its clockwise neighbor need to move. So instead of 90% reshuffling, you move just ~1/N of the data—dramatically less effort, faster recovery, smoother scaling.

But wait—what if the ring gets lopsided? One node ends up with a huge chunk of keys, becoming a hotspot? That’s where *virtual nodes* save the day. Instead of one physical node = one position on the ring, we assign it *dozens* of virtual positions—each a hash of the node ID plus a suffix (like ‘-v1’, ‘-v2’). Now, the ring looks like a mosaic of VNodes, and physical nodes share the ring more evenly. This smooths load distribution and makes rebalancing almost frictionless.

And while we’re at it—let’s talk about *federation*, the sibling pattern. Federation doesn’t split data—it *unifies* it. Imagine your company has separate HR, finance, and CRM databases, each managed independently. A federated layer lets users query them as one logical database, hiding the underlying heterogeneity. It’s like a travel booking site that pulls flight data from one airline, hotel from another, and car rental from a third—all behind a single search interface. The magic is in *transparency*: users don’t care where the data lives, just that it’s accessible. But federation has trade-offs: joins across sources are slow, and you’re dependent on each autonomous system’s uptime.

Now, here’s a real-world hook: when Netflix or Uber scales globally, they use sharding *and* consistent hashing to route requests, replicate data, and avoid catastrophic hotspots. And—importantly—they pair it with robust monitoring because debugging distributed transactions? Brutal. Remember the Saga pattern? It’s great for long-running workflows, but tracing failures across services, especially with cyclic dependencies or missing data isolation, can feel like detective work with half the clues missing.

So—final thought: sharding isn’t a silver bullet. It’s a *tool*. Use it when you need horizontal scale, geographic distribution, or better query isolation. But ask yourself: do you *really* need cross-shard joins? Are you prepared to handle rebalancing? If your data is highly interdependent, maybe federation—or even a well-tuned monolith—is smarter.

🤔 If you add one node to a 10-node cluster using naive modulo-based hashing, approximately what % of keys would need reassignment? How does consistent hashing improve this?

slides.title: N-Tier Architectures & Messaging Patterns
bullets: 🏗️ N-tier architectures separate components into physically isolated tiers—improving scalability and resilience but adding network latency.
🔄 Closed-layer architectures enforce strict layer-to-layer calls (e.g., tier 1 → tier 2 only), while open-layer architectures allow skipping tiers—balancing coupling vs. traffic.
✉️ Message brokers enable asynchronous, protocol-agnostic communication via point-to-point queues or pub/sub, decoupling services and enabling fault tolerance.
📦 Message queues ensure reliable, ordered, at-least-once delivery with features like dead-letter queues and backpressure, ideal for decoupled task processing.
📢 Pub/sub delivers messages instantly to all subscribers of a topic—enabling dynamic scaling, eliminating polling, and simplifying event-driven systems.
slides.example: A 3-tier e-commerce app: users interact via the presentation layer (web frontend), business logic validates orders (e.g., stock check), and the data layer writes to the database—each tier can scale independently.
slides.selfTestQuestion: 🤔 In a closed-layer 4-tier architecture, can tier 1 directly call tier 3? Why or why not?
slides.speakerNotes:
Alright, let’s dive into something foundational for building scalable, resilient cloud-native systems: *N-tier architectures* and the *messaging patterns* that make them sing. 

First—what *is* an N-tier architecture? It’s not just about code layers; it’s about *physical separation*. Imagine each tier—presentation, business logic, data access—running on its own dedicated server or cloud instance. That physical isolation? That’s what gives you scalability and fault tolerance. If your database tier crashes, your app tier might still serve cached content. But here’s the trade-off: every tier-to-tier call now crosses the network—adding latency. And remember: *layers* are logical groupings of code, while *tiers* are physical deployments. You *can* host multiple layers on the same tier (e.g., presentation + business logic on one VM), but separating them across tiers unlocks resilience.

Now, two flavors: *closed-layer* and *open-layer*. In closed-layer, each tier can *only* talk to the *next* one down—like a relay race where you must pass the baton hand-to-hand. This reduces coupling and enforces clean architecture, but it can cause unnecessary hops: tier 1 → tier 2 → tier 3 → tier 4, even if tier 1 *could* have gone straight to tier 4. In open-layer, tier 1 *can* call tier 4 directly—more efficient, but riskier: you might accidentally bypass validation in tier 2 or 3. It’s a design trade-off: strict control vs. performance.

Let’s ground this with a real-world analogy: think of a restaurant kitchen. The *presentation tier* is the waitstaff taking orders. The *business logic tier* is the head chef verifying ingredients, dietary rules, and portion sizes. The *data tier* is the inventory system updating stock. In a *closed-layer* setup, the waiter *must* hand the order to the sous-chef first, who then passes it to the head chef—maybe inefficient, but ensures no rules are skipped. In an *open-layer* setup, the waiter could sprint straight to the head chef—faster, but if the head chef’s distracted, orders might slip through unchecked.

Now—what happens when systems get *massive*? That’s where *messaging* comes in. A *message broker* is like a postal service for services: it receives, routes, stores, and delivers messages—even if the receiver is offline. Think RabbitMQ or NATS. It supports two core patterns: *point-to-point* (like a ticketing system: one job, one worker) and *publish-subscribe* (like a news wire: one alert, many departments act on it).

Crucially, *message queues* (e.g., SQS, RabbitMQ) are for *reliable, ordered, single-consumer* processing—ideal for order processing or background jobs. They support *at-least-once* delivery (retries on failure), *exactly-once* (via deduplication), and even *dead-letter queues* for poison messages that can’t be processed. But watch out: if your queue grows too large, it spills to disk, slowing everything down. That’s where *backpressure* saves the day—rejecting new requests when the queue’s full, so you don’t drown in a snowball effect.

Meanwhile, *pub/sub* is for *broadcast*—think real-time dashboards, notifications, or event-driven microservices. It’s *immediate*, *fan-out*, and *dynamic*: subscribers come and go, and the system adapts automatically. No polling! But unlike queues, pub/sub usually *doesn’t* guarantee delivery or track who got the message—it’s fire-and-forget for scale.

And how do brokers compare to *event streaming platforms* like Kafka? Brokers prioritize *reliability* (acknowledgments, retries, exactly-once). Event streams prioritize *throughput and ordering*—they’re built for high-volume, time-series event logs, but won’t help if you need guaranteed delivery per consumer.

Finally—why not use an *Enterprise Service Bus*? ESBs are monolithic, expensive, and hard to debug. Modern message brokers are *lightweight*, cloud-native, and perfect for microservices. They’re the Swiss Army knife, not the cathedral.

So—back to that self-test: in a *closed-layer* 4-tier system, tier 1 *cannot* call tier 3 directly—it *must* go through tier 2. That’s the definition of closed layering: sequential, adjacent-tier communication only. It’s about enforcing architectural discipline—even if it costs a bit of latency.

slides.title: ESB, Monoliths, Microservices & EDA
bullets: 🏗️ **Enterprise Service Bus (ESB)**: Centralized integration hub handling data transformation, routing, and protocol conversion—great for standardization but risks becoming a bottleneck or single point of failure.

📦 **Monoliths vs Modular Monoliths**: Monoliths are simple to build and debug but scale poorly and become hard to maintain; modular monoliths decouple code internally while deploying as one unit—offering a pragmatic middle ground.

🧩 **Microservices**: Loosely coupled, independently deployable services aligned to business capabilities—offering agility, fault isolation, and tech stack flexibility—but introduce distributed system complexity.

⚡ **Event-Driven Architecture (EDA)**: Services communicate via asynchronous events (publish-subscribe), enabling decoupling, scalability, and resilience—commonly implemented with Kafka, SNS, or Pub/Sub.

📝 **Event Sourcing ≠ EDA**: Event sourcing stores *state changes* as immutable events (the *source of truth*), while EDA uses events for *inter-service communication*—event sourcing is one *pattern* used *within* EDA.
slides.example: A retail system using Kafka to decouple order processing (producer) from inventory updates and email notifications (consumers), while storing each order’s lifecycle events (e.g., 'OrderPlaced', 'PaymentVerified', 'Shipped') in an event store for audit and replay.
slides.selfTestQuestion: 🤔 Which architecture avoids a single point of failure but introduces challenges like eventual consistency and complex error handling: ESB, Monolith, or Event-Driven Architecture?
slides.speakerNotes:
Alright, let’s unpack this rich landscape of architectural patterns—because choosing the right one can make or break your system’s scalability, maintainability, and team velocity.

First up: the **Enterprise Service Bus**, or ESB. Imagine an ESB as the central nervous system of your enterprise—like a bustling airport control tower. Every flight (service) must coordinate with the tower before takeoff or landing. It handles language translation (data transformation), flight routing (message routing), and even landing gear protocol checks (protocol conversion). That centralization *does* bring order: developers can plug in new services without rewriting legacy systems, and scaling can be done per component. But—and this is a big *but*—if the tower goes dark, *all* flights stop. One misconfigured integration can ripple across the entire system. And updating it? Think of it like upgrading air traffic control software mid-peak hour: high risk, high testing overhead, and cross-team coordination becomes a political nightmare. That’s why many organizations eventually see ESBs as bottlenecks—not because they’re flawed, but because they centralize too much control.

Now, contrast that with **monoliths**. Think of a monolith as a single, massive cathedral—everything’s under one roof, so communication is lightning-fast, debugging is straightforward, and ACID transactions are a breeze. But as the cathedral grows, adding a new chapel means tearing down part of the nave, and *every* upgrade requires shutting down the entire building. A single cracked arch (bug) can collapse the dome. That’s why many teams evolve toward **modular monoliths**: same cathedral, but now with clearly defined wings—kitchen, sanctuary, bell tower—each with its own internal architecture. You can renovate the kitchen without halting services in the sanctuary. It’s not microservices, but it’s a smart stepping stone.

Which brings us to **microservices**. Here, instead of one cathedral, you have a *city* of specialized buildings: a bakery (auth service), a post office (notification service), a marketplace (order service). Each is owned by a small team, deployed independently, and scales on its own. Need more checkout capacity? Scale the checkout microservice—not the whole app. But here’s the catch: now you need bridges between buildings (APIs), traffic management (service discovery), and disaster plans (circuit breakers). If the post office goes down, the bakery might still function—but if they share a common water supply (a shared database), suddenly *everything* is at risk. That’s why best practices emphasize *bounded contexts*, private data per service, and decentralized ownership. And yes—microservices solve *organizational* problems (like 50 teams stepping on each other’s toes), not just technical ones.

Which leads us to **Event-Driven Architecture (EDA)**—the glue that lets these services breathe independently. Imagine a pub-sub system like a social media feed: when you post (event producer), your followers (consumers) get notified, but you don’t know who’s following, and they don’t know each other. This decoupling enables massive scale—think Twitter handling millions of tweets per second. Tools like **Amazon SNS**, **Google Pub/Sub**, or **Apache Kafka** power this. But EDA isn’t free: you trade simplicity for complexity—handling out-of-order events, ensuring delivery, and debugging across time becomes an art. And here’s a crucial distinction: **Event sourcing** is *not* EDA. Event sourcing is about *how you store data*—instead of saving the current bank balance, you store every deposit and withdrawal. The *event log* is your truth. EDA is about *how services talk*. You can use event sourcing *within* a service *and* use EDA *between* services—it’s complementary, not competing.

So—should you go all-in on microservices? Ask yourself: *Is my team too big to ship on one codebase?* *Am I constantly blocked by other teams?* *Do I really need 24/7 global scale?* If you’re a startup with 5 engineers and a simple MVP? Start with a monolith. Netflix didn’t jump to microservices on day one—they iterated, hit scale pain points, *then* evolved. Architecture isn’t about copying giants—it’s about matching your structure to your business’s size, maturity, and goals.

So—final thought: the *best* architecture is the one that serves your *current* reality—not the one that looks sexiest on a tech blog.

slides.title: Event Sourcing, CQRS & API Patterns
bullets: ⚡ Event sourcing stores *all changes* as immutable events—ideal for audit trails, replayability, and real-time reporting.
🔄 CQRS separates read and write concerns: commands modify state, queries retrieve it—enabling independent scaling and optimization.
🧩 Event sourcing + CQRS = powerful combo: event log is the single source of truth; read models are denormalized projections.
🌐 API Gateway acts as a universal entry point—handling auth, routing, throttling, and more—simplifying client interactions with microservices.
🛠️ BFF pattern tailors backend logic per frontend (e.g., mobile, web), while GraphQL/gRPC/REST offer different trade-offs for API design.
slides.example: A financial trading platform uses event sourcing to log every trade, cancellation, and adjustment. With CQRS, high-volume trade writes scale independently from real-time dashboard reads. An API Gateway routes requests, enforces OAuth2, and throttles suspicious IPs—while a BFF for the trader’s mobile app aggregates trade history, balance, and alerts into one optimized response.
slides.selfTestQuestion: 🤔 If your system needs to reconstruct state from scratch after a catastrophic failure, which pattern guarantees full recovery—and why?
slides.speakerNotes: Alright, let’s unpack this powerful trio of patterns—Event Sourcing, CQRS, and modern API strategies—that are transforming how we build resilient, scalable systems. Imagine you’re building a banking app where *every action must be auditable*, *zero data loss is non-negotiable*, and *performance must adapt dynamically* to user traffic spikes. That’s where Event Sourcing shines. Instead of just storing the current account balance, you record *every* deposit, withdrawal, and transfer as an immutable event—like a blockchain for your domain. This means if your database crashes? No problem: you replay the event log, and *boom*—your system is fully restored, down to the last cent. Plus, you get real-time analytics out of the box: just stream events into a dashboard as they happen. But it’s not free: you need tight schema control—think Confluent Schema Registry—to avoid chaos when event formats evolve—and your network must be rock-solid, because every command generates an event that must be reliably persisted. Now, pair this with CQRS—the Command Query Responsibility Segregation pattern. Think of it like a restaurant: the kitchen (commands) *does* work, the waitstaff (queries) *only* fetches info. Commands change state (e.g., `TransferFundsCommand`), queries read it (e.g., `GetAccountBalanceQuery`). By splitting these, you optimize writes for consistency and reads for speed—maybe even using different databases. When combined with event sourcing, the event log *is* your write model, and your read model is a set of materialized views—like a denormalized dashboard view of account activity. The trade-off? Complexity: eventual consistency means your UI might lag slightly; duplicate messages can cause reprocessing headaches; and debugging is harder. But for high-compliance systems—think healthcare or finance—it’s worth it. Then enter the API layer. An API Gateway is your system’s bouncer: it handles authentication, rate limits, logging, and routes requests to the right microservice. Without it, clients would need to know which service to call—and manage retries, auth tokens, and circuit breakers themselves. But it *can* become a bottleneck if overloaded—so horizontal scaling is key. And when frontends are wildly different (e.g., a mobile app vs. a web admin panel), the Backend For Frontend (BFF) pattern shines: a dedicated backend per client type that aggregates, filters, and formats data. Ever seen a mobile app load 10 microservice calls just to show one screen? A BFF collapses that into one efficient call. And which API tech to use? REST is simple, universal, and cache-friendly—but suffers from over-fetching (getting too much data) or under-fetching (needing multiple round trips). GraphQL fixes that: the client *asks* for exactly what it needs, like requesting only `id`, `name`, and `city` instead of a whole user object. But it shifts complexity to the server and can trigger the N+1 query problem. gRPC? Think high-performance internal services: it uses Protocol Buffers—binary, lightning-fast, and strongly typed—and supports streaming, perfect for microservice-to-microservice comms. So ask yourself: when would you *not* use event sourcing? Maybe for simple CRUD apps where audit trails don’t matter. But for systems where history *is* the truth—like trading, IoT telemetry, or compliance-heavy domains—it’s transformative. And that’s why these patterns are shaping the future of cloud-native architecture.

slides.title: gRPC, Real-Time Comms, & Spatial Indexing
bullets: 💡 gRPC excels in low-latency, high-throughput microservices with bi-directional streaming and strong codegen—but has browser limits and a steeper learning curve.

📡 Long polling, WebSockets, and SSE each offer trade-offs for real-time server-to-client communication: polling is simple but inefficient, WebSockets enable full-duplex speed, and SSE provides lightweight unidirectional push.
🌍 Geohashing converts lat/long into compact, prefix-similar strings for spatial indexing—ideal for proximity searches and anonymized location sharing.
🌳 Quadtrees recursively subdivide 2D space into four quadrants, enabling efficient range queries and spatial indexing at scale—critical for maps and graphics.
⚡ Circuit breakers prevent cascading failures by halting requests to failing services after repeated errors, then cautiously test recovery in a 'half-open' state.
slides.example: San Francisco's coordinates (37.7564, -122.4016) → geohash '9q8yy9mf'; a 6-character geohash covers ~1.22 km²—perfect for city-level search without exposing exact coordinates.
slides.selfTestQuestion: 🤔 If your gRPC service is failing repeatedly, what state does the circuit breaker enter *immediately* after exceeding the failure threshold—and what happens to subsequent requests?
slides.speakerNotes:
Alright, let’s unpack this rich, powerful toolkit for modern distributed systems—starting with gRPC. You’ve heard of REST and GraphQL, but gRPC brings something special: Protocol Buffers as the data interchange format, compiled code generation, and *bi-directional streaming*—where both client and server can send messages concurrently over a single connection. That’s huge for real-time apps like live chat, gaming, or IoT telemetry. But here’s the catch: because it’s binary and uses HTTP/2 under the hood, browsers can’t talk to it natively (no Fetch API support), and it’s not human-readable like JSON. Plus, compared to REST, it’s newer, so tooling and community support are still maturing. Think of it like upgrading from a landline (REST) to a fiber optic line with two-way voice + video + data all at once (gRPC)—incredibly fast, but you need compatible hardware.

Now, for real-time comms: long polling is like asking your friend every 5 seconds, *‘Got anything for me yet?’*—they only reply when they have news. It works, but it’s wasteful. WebSockets? That’s like opening a dedicated phone line and keeping it open—you both talk freely, whenever you want. SSE is more like a radio broadcast: the server speaks, the client only listens. Great for stock tickers or notifications, but not for chat. Each has its niche.

Geohashing is genius: it turns messy decimal coordinates into clean, sortable strings. And here’s the magic—*prefix matching = proximity*. If two geohashes share the first 6 characters, those points are within ~1.22 km. So you can do substring comparisons instead of expensive Euclidean math. Want to find all users within 5 km? Just query for matching prefixes. Databases like Redis and Firestore have native support—this is production-grade.

Quadtrees take this further for *dynamic* spatial indexing. Imagine zooming into a map: as you zoom, the system only subdivides regions where points exist. No need to scan millions of coordinates—just traverse the tree. Used everywhere: Google Maps, Uber, even video game collision detection.

Finally, the circuit breaker: this isn’t just error handling—it’s *resilience engineering*. When a downstream service hogs resources and times out, every caller waits… until your whole system collapses. The circuit breaker detects this pattern, *opens* the circuit, and immediately fails fast—buying time for recovery. After a timeout, it enters *half-open*, tests one or two requests. If they succeed, it *closes* again. If not, it reopens. It’s like a smart fuse—protecting your system from self-inflicted meltdowns.

So—no silver bullets. REST for broad compatibility, gRPC for speed and streaming, WebSockets for bidirectional chat, geohashing for location, quadtrees for scale, and circuit breakers for resilience. Choose wisely based on your *domain*, not hype.

slides.title: Rate Limiting, Service Discovery & Disaster Recovery
bullets: 🛡️ Rate limiting protects systems from overload, DoS attacks, and runaway costs by capping request rates using algorithms like Leaky Bucket, Token Bucket, Fixed Window, and Sliding Log/Window.

🔄 In distributed systems, enforcing *global* rate limits across nodes is tricky—solutions include sticky sessions (low fault tolerance), centralized stores like Redis (latency/race risks), or atomic set-then-get patterns for scalability.

📍 Service discovery enables clients to locate dynamic microservice instances via client-side (direct registry query) or server-side (via load balancer) patterns, relying on highly available registries like Consul or etcd.
📊 SLAs (agreements), SLOs (objectives), and SLIs (measured metrics) form a feedback loop for reliability: SLIs quantify performance, SLOs set targets, and SLAs define business commitments.
🔥 Disaster recovery ensures business continuity after outages via strategies like cold/hot sites, guided by RTO (max downtime) and RPO (max data loss) targets to minimize impact and retain trust.
slides.example: A ride-hailing app uses token bucket rate limiting (100 req/s per user) to prevent a single user from overwhelming the driver-matching API during peak hours—while Redis tracks global limits across 10 service nodes using atomic INCR/EXPIRE to avoid race conditions.
slides.selfTestQuestion: 🤔 If your service has an SLO of 99.9% uptime and your SLI shows 99.85% over a month, are you in compliance—and what does that mean for your SLA?
slides.speakerNotes:
Alright, let’s unpack why rate limiting isn’t just a nice-to-have—it’s mission-critical infrastructure. Imagine you’re running a popular API for a food delivery app. One day, a botnet decides to flood your endpoint with 100,000 requests/second—maybe to stress-test, maybe for malicious reasons. Without rate limiting? Your database gets overwhelmed, auto-scaling kicks in aggressively, and your cloud bill spikes 10x overnight. Worse, real users can’t place orders. That’s where rate limiting acts like a bouncer at a club: it doesn’t *block* everyone, but it controls the *flow*—letting in only as many requests per second as your system can safely handle.

Now, how do we *do* this? The Leaky Bucket algorithm is like a bucket with a small hole at the bottom: requests fill the bucket, and they’re processed one per second as water drips out. If the bucket overflows? Requests get dropped—clean, predictable, and fair. The Token Bucket is more flexible: you get a bucket of tokens, refill them steadily, and each request consumes one token. No tokens? Wait. This lets you handle bursts *up to* your token capacity before throttling kicks in.

But here’s the kicker: in a distributed world with 50 microservices across 10 nodes, each node *thinking* it can handle 100 req/s means the *user* could hit 5,000 req/s total—bypassing your limit entirely. That’s why we need global rate limits. Sticky sessions (routing user A always to Node 3) are a quick fix but break when Node 3 dies. Centralized stores like Redis help—but naive read-increment-write operations cause race conditions: two requests read counter=99, both increment to 100, both write 100… and suddenly 200 requests slipped through! The fix? Atomic operations—like Redis’s `INCR` or `SETNX`—or smarter patterns like sliding window counters that weight recent vs older requests.

Moving to service discovery: in monoliths, services call each other like functions—easy. But in microservices? A payment service might spin up 10 instances today, 3 tomorrow, each with a new IP. How does the checkout service find them? That’s where service discovery shines. Client-side discovery means the client queries a registry (like Consul) to get live endpoints. Server-side discovery hides this behind a load balancer—simpler for clients, but adds a hop. Either way, the registry must be *highly available*—if it goes down, services can’t find each other, and the whole system stalls. Think of it like a dynamic phonebook that updates in real-time as people move houses.

Now, SLAs, SLOs, SLIs—they’re not just corporate jargon. Your SLA says, ‘We guarantee 99.9% uptime.’ That’s a *promise* to your users. The SLO is the *technical* target: ‘Uptime ≥ 99.9% over 30 days.’ And the SLI is the *actual measurement*: maybe you track `1 - (error_rate)`, where error_rate = failed_requests / total_requests. If your SLI dips below the SLO? You’re in breach—and that SLA might mean service credits or reputational damage. It’s a closed loop: measure (SLI), set goals (SLO), commit (SLA), improve.

Finally, disaster recovery. It’s not *if* but *when* something fails—a data center floods, a region goes dark, ransomware encrypts your backups. DR isn’t just ‘we have backups’—it’s about *recovery time* and *recovery point*. RTO is how fast you’re back online (e.g., 15 minutes). RPO is how much data you’re willing to lose (e.g., 1 minute of transactions). A cold site is cheap but slow to spin up; a hot site runs live replicas but costs 5x more. The goal? To turn a catastrophic outage into a brief, managed blip—so your customers never notice, and your business survives.

slides.title: Containers, OAuth, OIDC & SSO
bullets: 📦 Containers package apps with dependencies for consistent, isolated execution across environments.
🔐 OAuth 2.0 enables delegated authorization—users grant apps access to resources *without* sharing passwords.
👤 OpenID Connect (OIDC) builds on OAuth 2.0 to add user authentication and identity claims via JWTs.
🔄 SSO lets users log in once to access multiple apps via a trusted Identity Provider (IdP).
🔒 TLS/mTLS encrypt and authenticate communication—mTLS adds *mutual* verification for zero-trust systems.
slides.example: A web app uses Google (an IdP) via OIDC to authenticate users. After login, the app receives a JWT (access + identity token), then accesses Google APIs on the user’s behalf using OAuth 2.0 scopes—while the user’s containerized app runs identically on their laptop, staging, and production.
slides.selfTestQuestion: 🤔 In OAuth 2.0, which entity *actually owns* the protected resources and grants permission for access?
slides.speakerNotes:
Alright, let’s unpack this—because understanding containers, OAuth, OIDC, SSO, and TLS isn’t just buzzword bingo—it’s the backbone of modern secure, scalable systems. 🌐

First up: **containers**. Imagine you’re shipping a fragile, custom-built gadget. In the old days (virtual machines), you’d build a whole new room—complete with its own wiring, plumbing, and even a mini power plant—just to house that one gadget. 🏗️ Containers? They’re like standardized shipping containers: same size, same interface, same way of being loaded/unloaded—no matter if you’re in a warehouse, on a ship, or in a truck. They *virtualize the OS*, not the hardware—so they’re lightweight, fast to start, and share the host kernel. That means developers package *only* the app + its dependencies (say, Python 3.11 + NumPy 1.24), and ops teams deploy it anywhere—no more ‘but it works on my machine!’ 🚫💻➡️✅

Now—what about security and access? Enter **OAuth 2.0**. Think of it like a valet key for your car. You don’t give your *entire* key fob (your password!) to the valet—you give them *just enough* access: they can start the car and drive it, but can’t open the glovebox or trunk. That’s OAuth: *authorization*, not authentication. It lets apps act *on your behalf*—like letting a music app read your Spotify playlist—but never see your password. Key players: the *Resource Owner* (you), *Client* (the app), *Authorization Server* (issues tokens), and *Resource Server* (holds the data). Scopes define *exactly* what access you’re granting—like ‘read_profile’ vs ‘write_posts’.

But OAuth doesn’t tell you *who* the user is—just *what* they’re allowed to do. That’s where **OpenID Connect** steps in. It’s the thin layer on top of OAuth that adds identity. When you click “Sign in with Google,” OIDC kicks in: Google (now acting as an *OpenID Provider*) returns a JWT—containing your user ID, name, email—signed and verifiable. That JWT is your *identity assertion*. It’s why modern apps can log you in *and* fetch your profile in one smooth flow.

Which brings us to **SSO**—Single Sign-On. Ever logged into G Suite and instantly accessed Docs, Calendar, and Meet? That’s SSO in action. Behind the scenes, your browser is redirected to an Identity Provider (like Okta or Google Identity), you authenticate *once*, and the IdP issues a token (often via SAML or OIDC) that tells each app: *‘This user is verified—trust them.’* SAML uses XML and is common in legacy enterprise systems; OIDC uses JSON and is favored for web/mobile apps. SSO = convenience *and* security: fewer passwords, enforced MFA, and centralized deprovisioning when someone leaves the company.

Finally—**TLS and mTLS**. TLS is your encrypted tunnel—HTTPS is just TLS over HTTP. It ensures *confidentiality* (encryption), *integrity* (no tampering), and *authentication* (you’re talking to google.com, not a phishing site). But what if *both* sides need to prove who they are? Like two microservices talking inside a cluster? That’s where **mTLS** shines: both client *and* server present certificates, verify each other’s identity, and establish trust *mutually*. In zero-trust architectures, mTLS is non-negotiable—it prevents lateral movement if one service is compromised.

So—why does this all matter together? Because modern apps are *distributed*, *containerized*, *OAuth-protected*, *SSO-integrated*, and *TLS-secured* end-to-end. Mastering these concepts lets you design systems that are not just functional—but *secure*, *scalable*, and *maintainable*.

slides.speakerNotes:
System Design Interviews
System design is a very extensive topic and system design interviews are designed to evaluate your capability to produce technical solutions to abstract problems, as such, they're not designed for a specific answer. The unique aspect of system design interviews is the two-way nature between the candidate and the interviewer.

Expectations are quite different at different engineering levels as well. This is because someone with a lot of practical experience will approach it quite differently from someone who's new in the industry. As a result, it's hard to come up with a single strategy that will help us stay organized during the interview.

Let's look at some common strategies for system design interviews:

Requirements clarifications

System design interview questions, by nature, are vague or abstract. Asking questions about the exact scope of the problem, and clarifying functional requirements early in the interview is essential. Usually, requirements are divided into three parts:

Functional requirements

These are the requirements that the end user specifically demands as basic functionalities that the system should offer. All these functionalities need to be necessarily incorporated into the system as part of the contract.

"What are the features that we need to design for this system?"
"What are the edge cases we need to consider, if any, in our design?"
Non-functional requirements

These are the quality constraints that the system must satisfy according to the project contract. The priority or extent to which these factors are implemented varies from one project to another. They are also called non-behavioral requirements. For example, portability, maintainability, reliability, scalability, security, etc.

"Each request should be processed with the minimum latency"
"System should be highly available"
Extended requirements

These are basically "nice to have" requirements that might be out of the scope of the system.

"Our system should record metrics and analytics"
"Service health and performance monitoring?"
Estimation and Constraints

Estimate the scale of the system we're going to design. It is important to ask questions such as:

"What is the desired scale that this system will need to handle?"
"What is the read/write ratio of our system?"
"How many requests per second?"
"How much storage will be needed?"
These questions will help us scale our design later.

Once we have the estimations, we can start with defining the database schema. Doing so in the early stages of the interview would help us to understand the data flow which is the core of every system. In this step, we basically define all the entities and relationships between them.

"What are the different entities in the system?"
"What are the relationships between these entities?"
"How many tables do we need?"
"Is NoSQL a better choice here?"
API design

Next, we can start designing APIs for the system. These APIs will help us define the expectations from the system explicitly. We don't have to write any code, just a simple interface defining the API requirements such as parameters, functions, classes, types, entities, etc.

createUser(name: string, email: string): User
It is advised to keep the interface as simple as possible and come back to it later when covering extended requirements.

High-level component design

Now we have established our data model and API design, it's time to identify system components (such as Load Balancers, API Gateway, etc.) that are needed to solve our problem and draft the first design of our system.

"Is it best to design a monolithic or a microservices architecture?"
"What type of database should we use?"
Once we have a basic diagram, we can start discussing with the interviewer how the system will work from the client's perspective.

Now it's time to go into detail about the major components of the system we designed. As always discuss with the interviewer which component may need further improvements.

Here is a good opportunity to demonstrate your experience in the areas of your expertise. Present different approaches, advantages, and disadvantages. Explain your design decisions, and back them up with examples. This is also a good time to discuss any additional features the system might be able to support, though this is optional.

"How should we partition our data?"
"What about load distribution?"
"Should we use cache?"
"How will we handle a sudden spike in traffic?"
Also, try not to be too opinionated about certain technologies, statements like "I believe that NoSQL databases are just better, SQL databases are not scalable" reflect poorly. As someone who has interviewed a lot of people over the years, my two cents here would be to be humble about what you know and what you do not. Use your existing knowledge with examples to navigate this part of the interview.

Identify and resolve bottlenecks

Finally, it's time to discuss bottlenecks and approaches to mitigate them. Here are some important questions to ask:

"Do we have enough database replicas?"
"Is there any single point of failure?"
"Is database sharding required?"
"How can we make our system more robust?"
"How to improve the availability of our cache?"
Make sure to read the engineering blog of the company you're interviewing with. This will help you get a sense of what technology stack they're using and which problems are important to them.

URL Shortener
Let's design a URL shortener, similar to services like Bitly, TinyURL.

What is a URL Shortener?

A URL shortener service creates an alias or a short URL for a long URL. Users are redirected to the original URL when they visit these short links.

For example, the following long URL can be changed to a shorter URL.

Long URL: https://karanpratapsingh.com/courses/system-design/url-shortener

Short URL: https://bit.ly/3I71d3o

Why do we need a URL shortener?

URL shortener saves space in general when we are sharing URLs. Users are also less likely to mistype shorter URLs. Moreover, we can also optimize links across devices, this allows us to track individual links.

Our URL shortening system should meet the following requirements:

Functional requirements

Given a URL, our service should generate a shorter and unique alias for it.
Users should be redirected to the original URL when they visit the short link.
Links should expire after a default timespan.
Non-functional requirements

High availability with minimal latency.
The system should be scalable and efficient.
Extended requirements

Prevent abuse of services.
Record analytics and metrics for redirections.
Estimation and Constraints

Let's start with the estimation and constraints.

Note: Make sure to check any scale or traffic related assumptions with your interviewer.

This will be a read-heavy system, so let's assume a 100:1 read/write ratio with 100 million links generated per month.

Data model design

Next, we will focus on the data model design. Here is our database schema:

url-shortener-datamodel

Initially, we can get started with just two tables:

Stores user's details such as name, email, createdAt, etc.

Contains the new short URL's properties such as expiration, hash, originalURL, and userID of the user who created the short URL. We can also use the hash column as an index to improve the query performance.

What kind of database should we use?

Since the data is not strongly relational, NoSQL databases such as Amazon DynamoDB, Apache Cassandra, or MongoDB will be a better choice here, if we do decide to use an SQL database then we can use something like Azure SQL Database or Amazon RDS.

For more details, refer to SQL vs NoSQL.

Let us do a basic API design for our services:

Why do we need an API key?

As you must've noticed, we're using an API key to prevent abuse of our services. Using this API key we can limit the users to a certain number of requests per second or minute. This is quite a standard practice for developer APIs and should cover our extended requirement.

Now let us do a high-level design of our system.

Our system's primary goal is to shorten a given URL, let's look at different approaches:

In this approach, we can encode the original URL using Base62 which consists of the capital letters A-Z, the lower case letters a-z, and the numbers 0-9.
